description: 47-signal AEO/GEO scoring rubric for measuring whether a page will get cited by AI answer engines.

categories.label: Structure
categories.description: How parseable your page is to an AI engine. Schema, headings, lists, tables, alt-text, OG meta. The substrate that makes everything else legible.

categories.label: Authority
categories.description: Whether your page reads as authored by an expert, citing real sources. Bylines, citations, statistics, quotations, freshness, technical terms.

categories.label: Content
categories.description: Whether the page actually answers the buyer query — directly, completely, in the right register. BLUF, query coverage, sub-question coverage, readability.

categories.label: Trust
categories.description: Crawl-time + share-time trust signals: HTTPS, canonical, viewport, AI-bot access via robots/llms.txt, Twitter card, meta description.

categories.label: E-E-A-T
categories.description: Google's framework for Experience, Expertise, Authoritativeness, Trustworthiness. First-person evidence, case studies, author credentials, brand entity in Wikidata, press mentions, privacy policy, contact info.

signals.label: Structural depth (GEO-SFE)
signals.what: Heading hierarchy + nested structure depth — H1→H2→H3 nesting that mirrors a real outline.
signals.why: arXiv:2603.29979 shows a +17.3% citation lift for pages with measurable structural depth vs flat documents.
signals.howToFix: Group your content under named H2 subsections. Use H3 for sub-points. Don't skip levels.

signals.label: Internal linking
signals.what: Links from this page to other pages on your site — signal of a navigable graph.
signals.why: AI crawlers expand context via internal links the same way they crawl. Isolated pages get under-indexed.
signals.howToFix: Add 5–10 internal links per page to related concepts, glossary terms, or supporting pages.

signals.label: Page weight
signals.what: Total HTML size, lower is better. We flag pages over 500 KB.
signals.why: AI render bots have shorter timeouts than humans. Heavy pages get truncated or skipped.
signals.howToFix: Strip unused JS, defer below-the-fold images, kill render-blocking third-party scripts.

signals.label: JSON-LD presence
signals.what: Whether the page ships at least one schema.org JSON-LD block.
signals.why: Schema.org is the single highest-leverage signal for AI Overview eligibility — engines parse it before text.
signals.howToFix: Add at minimum an Organization or Article block. Our /tools/schema-generator outputs valid JSON-LD.

signals.label: JSON-LD validity
signals.what: Whether the JSON-LD parses, has @context = schema.org, and a valid @type.
signals.why: Malformed JSON-LD is worse than absent — engines log a parse error and downrank trust.
signals.howToFix: Run your JSON-LD through Google's Rich Results Test before shipping.

signals.label: JSON-LD type relevance
signals.what: Whether the @type matches the page intent (Article on a blog, Product on a PDP, FAQPage on a FAQ).
signals.why: Wrong-type schema gets ignored — Article markup on a homepage is parsed as noise.
signals.howToFix: Pick the closest schema.org type for the page's actual content, not a generic catch-all.

signals.label: FAQ schema
signals.what: FAQPage JSON-LD with at least 2 Q&A pairs.
signals.why: FAQPage is the highest single-type lift for AI Overview citations per Google's own SGE guidance.
signals.howToFix: Add FAQPage schema with 3–5 question/answer pairs that mirror real buyer queries.

signals.label: H1 quality
signals.what: Exactly one H1, length 20–70 chars, contains a primary query token.
signals.why: AI engines treat H1 as the page's claim — multiple H1s confuse the topical model.
signals.howToFix: One H1 per page, descriptive, 30–60 chars, includes the page's primary topic.

signals.label: H2 coverage
signals.what: ≥3 H2 subsections that segment the content for skim-readability.
signals.why: AI engines extract H2s as section anchors — pages without them are harder to summarize.
signals.howToFix: Break content into 3–6 H2 subsections, each addressing a sub-question.

signals.label: Lists
signals.what: ≥1 <ul> or <ol> with ≥3 items.
signals.why: Lists are the unit AI engines preferentially extract for 'how to X' and 'best Y' queries.
signals.howToFix: Convert at least one paragraph of enumerated reasons or steps into a real <ul>/<ol>.

signals.label: Tables
signals.what: ≥1 <table> with proper <thead>/<tbody> for structured data.
signals.why: Tables are the highest-extraction-rate format for comparison and spec-sheet queries.
signals.howToFix: If you have specs, prices, or comparisons, render them as real <table>s, not images.

signals.label: Alt-text
signals.what: ≥80% of tags have non-empty alt attributes.
signals.why: Multi-modal engines (Gemini, Claude, GPT-4o) read alt-text as page semantic context.
signals.howToFix: Add descriptive alt to every meaningful image. Decorative images get alt="".

signals.label: Open Graph metadata
signals.what: og:title, og:description, og:image, og:type all present.
signals.why: AI engine link previews + social shares use OG meta as the citation card.
signals.howToFix: Add the 4 OG tags. Use 1200×630 og:image. Our /tools/og-checker validates them.

signals.label: Outbound authority links
signals.what: Links to .edu, .gov, Wikipedia, peer-reviewed journals, or top-tier news sources.
signals.why: Linking out to trusted sources transfers authority backwards (citation graph effect).
signals.howToFix: Cite primary sources with real <a> tags, not bare-text references. Aim for 2–3 per long page.

signals.label: Citation density
signals.what: Inline citations per 1000 words (target ≥2).
signals.why: Pages that cite sources get cited as sources. Citation density is the strongest non-link authority signal.
signals.howToFix: Replace 'studies show' with 'a 2024 Stanford study (link)'. Specificity wins.

signals.label: Statistic density
signals.what: Quantitative claims with numbers per 1000 words (target ≥3).
signals.why: AI engines preferentially cite content with measurable claims over hand-wavy text.
signals.howToFix: Add real numbers — percentages, dollar amounts, time durations — to your claims.

signals.label: Direct quotations
signals.what: Inline <blockquote> or quoted attributions.
signals.why: Quotations imply primary research. They lift citation rates ~12% in the GEO paper test set.
signals.howToFix: Quote one customer, one expert, or one primary source per long page.

signals.label: Author byline
signals.what: A named author with at least a real name in the byline area.
signals.why: Anonymous content is treated as low-E-E-A-T. AI engines surface authored content.
signals.howToFix: Add 'By [Name]' at the top. Link to a real author bio page. Use Person schema.

signals.label: Date markup
signals.what: datePublished + dateModified in JSON-LD or <time datetime>.
signals.why: Engines penalize undated content as potentially stale. Dated content gets freshness scoring.
signals.howToFix: Add JSON-LD datePublished + dateModified, or a visible <time datetime> tag.

signals.label: Freshness
signals.what: Date modified within the last 365 days.
signals.why: Stale content gets downranked for time-sensitive queries. Re-publishing dates resets the clock.
signals.howToFix: Refresh top pages at least annually. Bump dateModified when you do.

signals.label: Technical term density
signals.what: Domain-specific terminology indicating real expertise vs surface-level writing.
signals.why: Pages that use precise technical vocabulary signal genuine expertise.
signals.howToFix: Use the field's actual technical language. Glossary-link or define the first occurrence.

signals.label: Direct answer to query
signals.what: Whether the buyer query appears answered in the first 200 chars.
signals.why: AI engines preferentially extract direct answers. Burying the answer = no citation.
signals.howToFix: Lead the page with a 1–2 sentence direct answer to the primary query. Then expand.

signals.label: BLUF (Bottom Line Up Front)
signals.what: A clear thesis statement in the first paragraph stating the page's main claim.
signals.why: Military-doctrine writing style — BLUF dramatically improves AI extractability.
signals.howToFix: Open with 'The answer is X. Here's why.' Don't bury the lede.
signals.source: U.S. military writing doctrine + adopted by Anthropic prompt eng team

signals.label: Query token coverage
signals.what: Fraction of query tokens that appear in the page body (target ≥80%).
signals.why: Coverage is a baseline relevance signal — missing tokens means the page misses the query.
signals.howToFix: Make sure every meaningful query token appears at least once in body content.

signals.label: Entity coverage
signals.what: Named entities mentioned that match the query's entity space.
signals.why: AI engines build an entity graph per page — missing related entities reduces topical authority.
signals.howToFix: If the page is about X, mention X's known related entities (people, places, products).

signals.label: Readability (Flesch)
signals.what: Flesch Reading Ease score (target 50–70 for general audiences).
signals.why: Engines extract from content humans can read. Sub-30 (academic) and 80+ (childlike) both underperform.
signals.howToFix: Shorten sentences. Cut adverbs. Aim for 16-word average sentence length.

signals.label: Page length
signals.what: Word count in the main content (target 800–3000).
signals.why: Too short = thin; too long = engines truncate. Sweet spot is medium-long form.
signals.howToFix: If under 600 words, expand with examples + sub-sections. If over 4000, split into hub + spokes.

signals.label: Information density
signals.what: Ratio of substantive content to filler.
signals.why: Dense content gets preferentially cited. Engines model 'value per token' implicitly.
signals.howToFix: Cut throat-clearing sentences. Replace 'It is important to note that' with the actual point.

signals.label: Mega-page coverage
signals.what: Whether the page covers multiple closely-related sub-topics under one URL.
signals.why: Mega-pages outrank thin pages on AI engines because they cover entity neighborhoods.
signals.howToFix: Consolidate 3 thin pages into one comprehensive page with H2-segmented sub-topics.

signals.label: YouTube embed
signals.what: Embedded YouTube video alongside the article.
signals.why: Multi-modal engines weight pages with on-topic video. 0.737 correlation with citation rate.
signals.howToFix: Create or embed a 2–4 minute video on the page topic. Mark up with VideoObject schema.

signals.label: Sub-query coverage
signals.what: Fraction of decomposed sub-questions answered in body content.
signals.why: Engines decompose seed queries into 4–6 sub-queries (Profound 'fanouts') — missing answers = missing citations.
signals.howToFix: Add a Q&A section that explicitly addresses the top 5 sub-questions for your topic.

signals.label: In-line definitions
signals.what: First-mention terms defined inline ('X (definition…)').
signals.why: Defined-in-place pages get cited as glossary sources. AI engines preferentially link to them.
signals.howToFix: Define the key 3–5 terms on first use, in-line.

signals.label: AI bot access (robots.txt)
signals.what: Whether robots.txt explicitly addresses GPTBot/ClaudeBot/PerplexityBot.
signals.why: Default-allow gets you crawled, but explicit allow signals intent + unlocks niche AI bots.
signals.howToFix: Add User-agent: GPTBot/ClaudeBot/OAI-SearchBot/PerplexityBot rules to robots.txt.

signals.label: llms.txt presence
signals.what: Whether /llms.txt exists per llmstxt.org spec.
signals.why: Explicit channel for telling AI engines exactly which content to ingest. 2026 frontier signal.
signals.howToFix: Generate llms.txt via our /audit/llms-txt tool. Add to site root.

signals.label: HTTPS
signals.what: Site responds correctly over TLS without mixed content.
signals.why: AI crawlers refuse to crawl mixed-content pages. HTTPS is table stakes.
signals.howToFix: Migrate to HTTPS. Force redirect with HSTS. Fix mixed-content warnings.

signals.label: Canonical URL
signals.what: <link rel=canonical> pointing to the page's preferred URL.
signals.why: Without canonical, AI engines may split citations across duplicate URLs and downrank each.
signals.howToFix: Add <link rel=canonical href=…> to every page. Self-canonical on canonical pages.

signals.label: Viewport meta
signals.what: <meta name=viewport content=…> present.
signals.why: Mobile rendering signal — AI crawlers favor mobile-first content.
signals.howToFix: Add <meta name=viewport content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1">.

signals.label: Twitter card
signals.what: <meta name=twitter:card> + twitter:image present.
signals.why: AI engines fall back to Twitter card on platforms without OG support.
signals.howToFix: Add twitter:card=summary_large_image + twitter:image (can reuse og:image).

signals.label: Meta description
signals.what: <meta name=description> with 80–160 chars of summary text.
signals.why: AI engines use meta description as a primary summary candidate when generating cites.
signals.howToFix: Write a 130-char meta description for every page. Include the primary query.

signals.label: First-person evidence
signals.what: First-person language indicating real experience ('we tested', 'I built').
signals.why: Google's E-E-A-T update prioritizes lived experience. First-person signals it.
signals.howToFix: Use first-person sparingly but precisely — 'we tested', 'in our experience', 'I found that…'.

signals.label: Case study evidence
signals.what: Named customer outcomes, numbers, or before/after evidence in-line.
signals.why: Case studies are the highest-conversion E-E-A-T signal — prove it, don't just claim it.
signals.howToFix: Name one customer. Quote one outcome with numbers. 'We helped X go from Y → Z.'

signals.label: Author credentials
signals.what: Author has visible credentials — years of experience, prior role, education, certifications.
signals.why: Anonymous bylines fail E-E-A-T. Credentialed authors get treated as expert sources.
signals.howToFix: Each author page lists credentials, prior roles, years in field, links to LinkedIn/Twitter.

signals.label: Byline depth
signals.what: Byline links to a real author bio page with sufficient detail.
signals.why: Byline → bio page → credentialed author chain is the E-E-A-T graph engines trace.
signals.howToFix: Make every byline link to /authors/[name] with bio, photo, credentials, prior work.

signals.label: Brand entity in Wikidata
signals.what: Brand has a Wikidata entity (Q-number) that AI engines can dereference.
signals.why: Wikidata is the canonical entity database — engines use it to validate brand identity. No entity = no trust anchor.
signals.howToFix: Get a Wikidata entry: requires 2–3 press mentions as citations. Submit at wikidata.org.

signals.label: Press mentions
signals.what: References to or links from top-tier press (NYT, WaPo, Bloomberg, FT, etc.).
signals.why: Press mentions transfer authority across the citation graph and validate brand entity claims.
signals.howToFix: Earn press. Display logos. Link back to the original article (not your case study).

signals.label: Privacy + terms
signals.what: Linked privacy policy + terms of service from every page.
signals.why: YMYL (Your-Money-Your-Life) categories require these — and engines treat them as legitimacy signals everywhere.
signals.howToFix: Footer-link privacy and terms on every page. Real pages, not coming-soon stubs.

signals.label: Contact info
signals.what: Real contact info — email, phone, or address visible on the page or linked /contact.
signals.why: Anonymous brands fail E-E-A-T. Real contact info signals real entity behind the content.
signals.howToFix: Add an email + physical address (real or registered office) to the footer.
